FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, Baseline N=11,878 from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study.

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone variable: smartphone ownership (age of acquisition). Applies B1 (smartphone ownership measured separately).

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression analysis testing smartphone ownership → parent-child informant discrepancy in internalizing problems (b = -0.21, SE = 0.042, p < 0.001, 95% CI [-0.29, -0.23]).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients and statistics reported: regression β and SE for main effect; Figure 1 shows multi-group latent growth curve analyses with coefficients (β = 29**, β = -24*, etc.) and p-values.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD longitudinal data across 4 waves (Years 1-4) examining smartphone ownership impact on parent-child discrepancy.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Development and Psychopathology (2025); no evidence of preprint/abstract duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone is NOT the dependent variable. The dependent variable is parent-child informant discrepancy of internalizing problems. Smartphone is the independent variable. This is SM/smartphone → non-SM-outcome analysis, so FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Clear longitudinal design with smartphone ownership as predictor and internalizing problem discrepancy as outcome. Multi-group models test moderation by smartphone ownership status.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study, T1 N=11,787 US adolescents followed longitudinally.

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying electronic media variables: overall electronic media use + specific types including social electronic media (social SM). Applies B1 (separate β for overall and SM-specific types).

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal regression examining bidirectional associations between electronic media use and family conflict across 3 years using cross-lagged panel model.

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients reported in results and supplementary materials; model fit indices provided (CFI, TLI, RMSEA, SRMR).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD longitudinal data (T1–T4) examining electronic media use ↔ family conflict associations.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Journal of Family Psychology (2025); no evidence of preprint/abstract duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Electronic media/social media is used as both IV (predicting family conflict) and as a DV in cross-lagged models (B20 — bidirectional/cross-lagged design). FT-EC7 does not apply per B20 rule. Additionally, family conflict is a non-demographic outcome, so even SM→family conflict analysis alone would disqualify FT-EC7.
notes: Bidirectional associations tested; electronic media use → family conflict was significant, but reverse was not. Gender differences examined (girls' social media use patterns especially disruptive).

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, Baseline N~12,000, age M=9.9; representative US sample (White=52.0%, Hispanic=20.3%, Black=15.0%, Asian=2.1%, Other=10.5%).

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying social media variable: time on social media (measured across 4 years). Applies B1 (SM measured with separate coefficient in growth models).

FT_IC3.evidence: Parallel process latent growth curve models testing association between social media use trajectory and cognitive performance trajectories (crystallized abilities, inhibitory control/attention, processing speed). Path coefficients estimated.

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients and model fit statistics reported in tables (Table 1: univariate growth models with β, SE, p-values; Table 2: bivariate correlations). Model fit indices: CFI, TLI, SRMR, RMSEA.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD longitudinal data over 4 years examining social media use ↔ cognitive performance trajectory coupling.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Journal of Research on Adolescence (2025); no evidence of preprint/abstract duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media is the IV (predicting cognitive performance trajectories), not the DV. Analysis examines SM ↔ non-SM-outcome (cognitive abilities). FT-EC7 does not apply because SM is not modeled as a dependent variable predicted by demographics alone.
notes: Parallel process latent growth curve design; social media trajectory negatively coupled with crystallized abilities and inhibitory control/attention trajectories, positively coupled with processing speed trajectory.
